metadata.title: Why Some Light an Atom Should Emit Is Forbidden

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Hydrogen's two-p state decays in about a nanosecond, while two-s survives roughly a tenth of a second. The energy gap exists, but the leading radiative channel is forbidden.

props.command: Why can an energetically possible atomic photon still be forbidden?
props.segment: Why Some Atomic Light Is Forbidden
props.runningText: checking electric-dipole selection rules and decay channels…
output: Two-photon decay provides the slow route.

beats.narration_text: Allowed electric-dipole transitions in hydrogen commonly occur on nanosecond scales. The two-p to one-s transition is the nearby benchmark.
The two-s state lies about ten point two electron volts above one-s, yet its lifetime in isolated hydrogen is about zero point one two seconds. It is metastable, not permanently stable.
Why can two-s not simply emit one photon and fall to one-s? Energy conservation allows the photon energy. The transition amplitude is blocked by symmetry and angular-momentum selection rules.
An energy gap is necessary for spontaneous emission, but not sufficient. The electromagnetic interaction's leading electric-dipole matrix element must also connect the initial and final states.

For an electric-dipole, or E-one, transition, the orbital selection rule is delta ell equals plus or minus one, with a parity change. This is the dominant single-photon channel for ordinary atomic decays.
Both two-s and one-s have ell zero and even parity. Their delta ell is zero, so the electric-dipole matrix element vanishes. The two-s to one-s E-one transition is forbidden.
Two-p has ell one and odd parity. Falling to one-s gives delta ell minus one and changes parity, so the E-one channel is allowed. Two-s fails both E-one signatures.

Two-s escapes mainly through simultaneous two-photon emission via intermediate virtual states. The combined two-photon angular momentum and parity can satisfy the total transition rules. The second-order process is much slower than one-photon E-one emission.
That long lifetime makes two-s useful for precision spectroscopy. The one-s to two-s transition can be driven by two photons, enabling exceptionally narrow and precise hydrogen measurements.

Forbidden does not mean impossible. It means forbidden in a specified approximation or multipole channel. Here the leading one-photon electric-dipole route vanishes, while the two-photon route remains.
Your turn. Compare two-p to one-s and two-s to one-s. Which has delta ell plus or minus one and a parity change? Two-p to one-s, so it is E-one allowed.
Why Some Light an Atom Should Emit Is Forbidden. Liam, in for Bear.
